title: A nanoscale magnetic spectrum analyzer based on qubit dressed states
abstract: Magnetic field fluctuations on nanometer length scales manifest in a diverse range of phenomena -- electron and spin dynamics in materials and devices, quantum many-body systems, and molecular chemistry. Measuring these phenomena requires sensors with a challenging combination of broad spectral bandwidth, high sensitivity, and nanoscale spatial resolution. Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers, atom-like quantum sensors in diamond, possess the requisite sensitivity and nanoscale sensing volume, but are typically limited in bandwidth by the practical speed of the applied quantum control sequence. Here, we overcome this limitation by exposing the NV qubit to a microwave dressing field during a dynamical decoupling sequence, which both amplifies and frequency-mixes target signals at arbitrary frequencies into the detection band of the dynamical decoupling protocol. We demonstrate this approach by using NV centers to detect both coherent and noisy nanoscale spin wave dynamics in a magnetic yttrium-iron-garnet (YIG) thin film over a broad frequency range. Our technique generalizes to other qubit platforms, providing a versatile framework for nanoscale spectroscopy across diverse physical and chemical systems.

bite.hook: A new quantum 'dressing' technique lets diamond sensors analyze high-frequency magnetic signals at the nanoscale.
tldr: They developed a nanoscale magnetic spectrum analyzer to overcome the speed limits of conventional quantum control sequences.
They exposed a diamond nitrogen-vacancy qubit to a microwave dressing field during a dynamical decoupling sequence.
They successfully detected both coherent and noisy spin wave dynamics in a magnetic thin film over a broad frequency range.
bite.whyItMatters: Standard quantum sensors are too slow to detect high-frequency magnetic signals. This technique acts like a frequency converter, opening up nanoscale chemical analysis and material science at previously unreachable speeds.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to film a humming bird's wings with a slow camera. By using a special microwave 'strobe light' effect, the researchers slow down the fast magnetic vibrations so their quantum camera can easily capture them.

glossary.shortDef: Quantum states of a system that are modified by a continuous external electromagnetic field.
glossary.eli5Def: A quantum particle that is constantly bathed in light or microwaves, changing how it behaves and reacts to the world.

glossary.shortDef: A technique that uses timed pulses to protect a quantum state from environmental noise.
glossary.eli5Def: Flipping a quantum coin back and forth very quickly so that random outside noise doesn't ruin its state.

glossary.shortDef: A propagating disturbance in the magnetization of a magnetic material.
glossary.eli5Def: A wave of magnetic tipping that travels through a material, like a 'wave' created by fans in a sports stadium.
